video_title: The Fake World Is Breaking | Thomas Pacchia
gist: PubKey founder Thomas Pacchia and Peter McCormack argue society is transitioning from a 'fake world' to a 'real world,' with the actionable investment theses being Bitcoin self-custody, locally-run/jailbroken decentralized AI, and physical third-place community (churches and bars). Much of the ~72-minute conversation is political and philosophical, but it carries concrete signal on Bitcoin's shift from sovereignty to financialized asset, an emerging AI surveillance/access-concentration stack (Anthropic/OpenAI -> Oracle -> Palantir), and UK fiscal/productivity collapse.

insights.claim: Thomas Pacchia argues Bitcoin's self-sovereignty mission is now in a 'really precarious state' because ETFs and treasury companies pushed holders to treat Bitcoin as an appreciating asset rather than something to self-custody.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Signals the sovereignty/self-custody narrative is losing share to the ETF/treasury 'number-go-up' framing; watch self-custody tooling adoption vs custodial product flows as a divergence.
insights.quote: the self-s sovereignty mission is, I think, in a really precarious state for Bitcoin.

insights.claim: Pacchia says the last two years of ETFs and treasury companies made people focus on Bitcoin as an asset that goes up in value rather than on the self-sovereignty properties, and framed a coming 'second bite at the apple' for building sovereign/parallel systems.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Implies a coming rotation in Bitcoin narrative back toward self-custody/privacy tooling, a potential tailwind for wallets, nodes, and privacy infrastructure ventures like PubKey.

insights.claim: Pacchia describes Bitcoin as 'very fragile' but the closest existing thing to an unstoppable parallel system that can out-compete captured institutions, calling it 'a candle in a very large dark cave.'
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Frames the long-term bull case as institutional-failure hedge rather than momentum; positions Bitcoin plus decentralized AI as the two assets to own if you expect state/institutional collapse.
insights.quote: Bitcoin is very fragile but it is you know a candle in a very large dark cave.

themes.theme: Decentralized and locally-run AI as parallel system

insights.claim: Pacchia argues locally-run, jailbroken open-source LLMs and decentralized AI are one of the only optimistic pathways and a 'parallel system' that could out-compete captured institutions.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Investable thesis: on-device/local inference, open-weight model tooling, and privacy-preserving AI infra as the 'parallel system' trade alongside Bitcoin.
insights.quote: jailbreaking jailbreaking uh LLMs and models is I think the only way out.

insights.claim: Pacchia warns that using centralized models sends user data to Anthropic and OpenAI, which can flow through Oracle and Palantir to expand a 'surveillance panopticon,' making local models the privacy-preserving alternative.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Names an explicit AI surveillance data-flow stack (model providers -> Oracle -> Palantir); relevant to how privacy-focused investors view exposure to centralized AI vendors and government-data integrators.
insights.quote: so that it doesn't just go to anthropic open AI and they can gobble it up and put it through you know Oracle and Palunteer

insights.claim: Pacchia notes that not everyone is comfortable self-custodying Bitcoin or running a node, and that AI is 'speedrunning' the same last-decade adoption challenges, meaning local AI faces a high technical barrier to mass adoption.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: The bottleneck for the decentralized-AI trade is usability, not capability; ventures that lower the self-hosting learning curve capture the adoption gap.

insights.claim: Pacchia states DeepSeek can be run locally and reprogrammed, offering open-source models people can put on their own desktops as a route around platform censorship.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Points to open-weight Chinese models as viable local-inference substrates; relevant to the open vs closed model competitive dynamic.

themes.theme: AI model access concentration and reliability

insights.claim: The hosts assert the most capable AI models are being released only to select users, and Pacchia says this can be simultaneously about hoarding a powerful tool and a genuine national-security risk.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Frames frontier-model gating as a deliberate access-concentration dynamic; investors should watch tiered/gated model release as a signal of where real capability sits versus public products.

insights.claim: Pacchia reports noticing recent AI platform performance degradation ('Cloud's also been kind of garbage lately... degradation across the entire platform') but explicitly disclaims knowledge of its veracity.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Anecdotal reliability complaints, self-flagged as unverified; treat as weak sentiment signal on frontier-model service quality, not a data point.
insights.quote: Cloud's also been kind of garbage lately. It slowed down. There was degra degradation across the entire like platform.

insights.claim: Pacchia claims government systems holding sensitive information are 'probably pretty vulnerable,' hinting at underappreciated cyber/security exposure.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Implied tailwind for security/hardening spend if state systems are as exposed as claimed, though offered without specifics.

insights.claim: McCormack describes a UK £100k wage cliff: crossing £100k costs the ~£10k tax-free allowance and there is effectively no point earning between £100k and £115k, so high earners near £99k ask for more time off instead of pay rises, suppressing productivity.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: Concrete marginal-tax distortion that caps UK high-skill labor output; relevant to UK growth, wage inflation, and the case against long-GBP/UK domestic productivity.
insights.quote: there's no point earning anything between 100 and 115 because your takeover is the same and then even if you get over that you start hitting the 55% tax.

insights.claim: McCormack claims UK welfare can pay roughly £70,000 a year to a non-working person with three children, arguing benefits are set high enough to disincentivize work.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: If accurate, a structural labor-supply drag; feeds the bear case on UK fiscal sustainability and productivity.
insights.quote: If I don't work, I have three kids. I can get £70,000 a year.

insights.claim: McCormack predicts the UK is heading 'harder left' with Andy Burnham likely to replace Starmer without a leadership contest or election, bringing higher taxes and more debt, and warns the country could become 'some weird combination of Argentina South Africa and Lebanon.'
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: Bearish macro call on UK gilts/GBP and domestic assets; flags political continuity of tax-and-spend as the base case.

insights.claim: McCormack argues nothing in the UK public sector (roads, NHS, defense, borders, education) has improved in 15 years under both Conservative and Labour governments, and VAT was added to private school fees, pushing families out of better-performing private education.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: Cross-party public-service decline reframed as a state-capacity problem, not a left/right one; underpins the bearish UK institutional thesis.

insights.claim: McCormack cites UK HS2 as roughly £100bn (or tens of billions) spent 'achieving nothing,' contrasted with China building high-speed rail at scale in the same period, and a proposed Heathrow runway that would take 15-20 years mostly spent on consultants and judicial reviews.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: Illustrative of UK infrastructure-delivery dysfunction; supports the low-growth, high-cost-of-capital-projects view of the UK.

insights.claim: Pacchia argues the US has institutional 'agility' to change policy direction faster than the UK, France, Germany, and Italy, which are 'turning the Titanic,' but warns this advantage only matters with staying power beyond a single four-year cycle.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Relative-institutions framework favoring US adaptability over European rigidity; a lens for cross-country policy-risk positioning.

insights.claim: Pacchia frames a populist far-left DSA faction (AOC, Mamdani) forming after Bernie Sanders, noting Mamdani can claim three victories in the recent New York primaries, with an analogous libertarian/anarchist-right faction (Thomas Massie) potentially breaking from the GOP/MAGA.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Both major US parties fragmenting toward populist wings; relevant to policy-volatility, NYC governance risk, and the durability of current market-friendly agendas.

insights.claim: Both hosts lament that Doge, framed as an AI-driven effort to identify wasteful government spending, was effectively abandoned after about 10 months rather than being given four years to run.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Reads the wind-down as a signal that entrenched spending interests reassert quickly; bearish on near-term US fiscal-tightening follow-through.

insights.claim: Pacchia predicts intra-party conflict will spread, citing the left 'eating itself' (Scott Wiener, in the mix for Pelosi's seat, harassed in public over his Israel/Palestine stance) and expects a conservative-eating-conservative faction to ferment next.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Rising intra-coalition instability raises US political-risk premium and unpredictability of policy outcomes.

insights.claim: McCormack and Pacchia converge on a framework that the bigger the 'surface area' of the state, the bigger the surface area for grift and corruption, arguing the state should be as small as possible.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model tying state size to rent-seeking; underpins their pro-parallel-systems, small-government investment worldview.
insights.quote: the bigger the surface area of the state, the more chance there is for grifted corruption.

insights.claim: Pacchia relays Nick Carter's point that libertarian politics is self-defeating because succeeding requires accumulating power, which is antithetical to libertarianism.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Explains why the answer they reach is exit/parallel systems (Bitcoin, local AI) rather than electoral politics.
insights.quote: The problem with libertarian politics is that uh for it to be successful, it has to accumulate power, which is antithetical to being libertarian.

insights.claim: McCormack cites Michael Malice's framing that viewing the state as rival gangs competing for territory makes politics easier to understand.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: A cynical-realist lens for interpreting policy as territorial rent extraction rather than public service.

insights.claim: Pacchia's stated thesis is that two institutions emerge from the crisis, churches and bars/pubs, with Gen Z returning to organized religion in a 'barbell' (older congregants plus the very young, almost nobody in the middle); he acknowledges he is 'talking his own book' as owner of PubKey bars in New York and Washington.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: A physical 'third place' / in-person community thesis he is personally invested in via PubKey; watch Gen Z religious attendance and community-venue formats as the counter-trend to digital isolation.
insights.quote: churches and bars, a return to the traditional role of pubs and taverns where you can meet and discuss really complicated topics in person

insights.claim: Pacchia argues a strong male base within a country makes population compliance harder to achieve, linking 'Boy Scouts' scandals and 'sports sanitization' to a broader thesis about the erosion of male social structures.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: A cultural-thesis lens rather than a trade; relevant only as context for his 'rebuild in-person male community' worldview.

themes.theme: AI political tooling and information markets

insights.claim: McCormack built an AI tool called 'Show Us Receipts' that ingested every tweet a politician wrote and fact-checked them (true / partly true / misleading / false / unclassifiable); grading just the last 50 tweets of every politician cost about $5,000 in tokens, with a full run estimated at $50,000-$150,000.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: Concrete cost curve for large-scale LLM reasoning applications ($5k for 50 tweets each), useful for sizing the economics of AI content-analysis products.
insights.quote: I just took the last 50 tweets of every politician. It cost me five grand. If I want to do all of them, it may be 50 150 grand

insights.claim: McCormack sketches scaling an AI fact-check service from politicians' tweets to TV shows (Question Time), political podcasts, news articles, and government contracts (who was awarded, who awarded it, are they Facebook friends), but insists it must run on censorship-resistant/decentralized platforms rather than Anthropic, OpenAI, Facebook, or even X.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: Identifies a decentralized 'source of truth' information-transparency product category; the platform-risk argument is that centralized AI/social hosts can censor or shadowban it.

insights.claim: McCormack says he has not released Show Us Receipts partly because UK libel law exposes him to a lawsuit if the AI mis-grades a statement as a lie, whereas in the US he could publish with a disclaimer.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: Jurisdictional/regulatory arbitrage: UK defamation risk chills AI-media products that would ship freely in the US; a real constraint on where such ventures domicile.

insights.claim: Pacchia predicts an imminent societywide 'AI psychosis' epidemic that he expects to be far worse than the COVID loneliness epidemic, driven by AI precisely targeting what enrages or isolates individuals.
insights.speaker: Thomas Pacchia (guest, PubKey founder)
insights.so_what: Frames AI-driven personalization as a societal-harm vector; relevant to regulatory backlash risk and to demand for the in-person/decentralized alternatives he champions.
insights.quote: the AI societywide AI psychosis that is just around the corner, it's probably starting to begin is going to be a million times worse than the uh the COVID loneliness epidemic.

insights.claim: McCormack warns AI could just as easily flood the world with more propaganda as with trusted information, raising the core question of whether users can still tell the source of truth.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: Two-sided view of AI content: the same generative capacity powers both verification tools and propaganda at scale, keeping provenance/authenticity a key unsolved (and investable) problem.
